SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper builds a coherent variational objective and derives a practical batch selection criterion, and presents experiments on MNIST and USPS that show gains over random/uncertainty baselines. However several key steps use uncontrolled or loose approximations (e.g. treating the MLE as the true parameter, approximating determinants and traces by functions of traces, replacing Fisher inverse traces by inverses of traces) without theoretical guarantees (sections 3.3–3.5 and Eqns. 12–16). The experimental methodology is limited (only MNIST/USPS, few baselines, no statistical tests) so empirical support is modest. Overall the method is plausible but has nontrivial unsupported assumptions and derivation gaps that temper confidence in correctness.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The work combines variational free-energy style reasoning (Graves 2011) and asymptotic MLE Gaussian approximations with modern Kronecker-factored Fisher approximations (KFAC) to produce a scalable batch active learning criterion for deep CNNs, which is a novel synthesis. Prior elements (variational Bayesian NN, KFAC, batch/submodular selection) exist individually, but applying this particular chain of approximations to produce a backprop-free, Fisher-based batch selection appears to be a noteworthy and non-trivial extension. The paper clearly positions itself against related work (section 2) and claims a distinct practical contribution.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in a standard structure (intro, related work, derivation, greedy algorithm, experiments, discussion), and the main contributions are stated early. Nevertheless several derivation details are omitted or hand-waved (e.g. the internal form of ψ and τ is left undescribed in 3.2, many approximations are stated without formal justification), there are repeated/uneven passages and missing figures/tables references, and some notation/approximations are introduced abruptly, making parts of the exposition hard to follow. Overall readable but would benefit from clearer derivations and complete definitions.
